metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the 'reconciler-function-extraction' companion ticket exists (add its ticket id to this plan) or include apply_inbound.py relief directly in this ticket's scope.
findings.finding: The scoping exclusion 'apply_inbound.py is separately relieved by the reconciler-function-extraction ticket' references a work item that has zero traces in the repository — no file, branch, ticket id, or in-code comment matching 'reconciler-function-extraction' was found. This is a G-4 scope exclusion: if the referenced ticket does not exist or does not land, apply_inbound.py remains at its 800-LOC cap with no coverage from this ticket or any verifiably linked ticket.
findings.location: Finding section — 'Note: `apply_inbound.py` is separately relieved by the reconciler-function-extraction ticket'
findings.suggested_fix: Add the concrete ticket id (e.g. `see ticket <id>`) for the reconciler-function-extraction work, or add apply_inbound.py to the Remediation and Acceptance Criteria of this ticket.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC (or extend the cascade_flag note) requiring that private symbols accessed by oracle.py (`_OPENGREP_CANDIDATES`, `_ASTGREP_CANDIDATES`, `_METRIC_CANDIDATES`, `_GITLEAKS_CANDIDATES`, `_resolve_binary`, `_binary_version`) remain importable from `engine_b` after the split — either by staying in that module or via an explicit re-export.
findings.finding: The plan's cascade_flag says 'check importers' but the ACs do not cover private-symbol re-exports: oracle.py references six engine_b-private symbols (engine_b._OPENGREP_CANDIDATES, engine_b._ASTGREP_CANDIDATES, engine_b._METRIC_CANDIDATES, engine_b._GITLEAKS_CANDIDATES, engine_b._resolve_binary, engine_b._binary_version) that may move to the new sibling module, silently breaking oracle.py at runtime with no AC to catch it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria / cascade_flag note; src/rebar/grounding/oracle.py lines 361-368
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ACs: 'Private symbols accessed by oracle.py (_OPENGREP_CANDIDATES, _ASTGREP_CANDIDATES, _METRIC_CANDIDATES, _GITLEAKS_CANDIDATES, _resolve_binary, _binary_version) remain importable from rebar.grounding.engine_b (re-exported if extracted to a sibling).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate oracle.py's six private-symbol accesses on engine_b (_OPENGREP_CANDIDATES, _ASTGREP_CANDIDATES, _METRIC_CANDIDATES, _GITLEAKS_CANDIDATES, _resolve_binary, _binary_version) in the cascade_flag and add an AC or note constraining which symbols may move vs. must stay / be re-exported.
findings.finding: oracle.py (inside the same grounding/ dir but a distinct module) directly accesses six engine_b private symbols by attribute — engine_b._OPENGREP_CANDIDATES, engine_b._ASTGREP_CANDIDATES, engine_b._METRIC_CANDIDATES, engine_b._GITLEAKS_CANDIDATES, engine_b._resolve_binary, engine_b._binary_version — none of which are enumerated in the plan's cascade_flag or ACs. If any of these land in the extracted sibling module, oracle.py will raise AttributeError at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the cascade_flag: 'oracle.py accesses engine_b._OPENGREP_CANDIDATES, ._ASTGREP_CANDIDATES, ._METRIC_CANDIDATES, ._GITLEAKS_CANDIDATES, ._resolve_binary, ._binary_version — these must stay in engine_b or be re-exported from it.' Add a matching AC: 'oracle.contract() passes its existing test suite after the split.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name oracle.py explicitly in the cascade_flag: it accesses six private symbols directly from engine_b (_OPENGREP_CANDIDATES, _ASTGREP_CANDIDATES, _METRIC_CANDIDATES, _GITLEAKS_CANDIDATES, _resolve_binary, _binary_version) and must either keep those names in engine_b or gain re-exports after any split.
findings.finding: oracle.py accesses six private symbols directly from engine_b (engine_b._OPENGREP_CANDIDATES, engine_b._ASTGREP_CANDIDATES, engine_b._METRIC_CANDIDATES, engine_b._GITLEAKS_CANDIDATES, engine_b._resolve_binary, engine_b._binary_version). The plan's cascade_flag says 'check importers' generically, but the implementer will not know that oracle.py is the one importer that pierces the module-private boundary with underscore-prefixed names. Moving any of these to a sibling without re-exporting from engine_b will produce a silent AttributeError at runtime in oracle.py's backend-availability probe path.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to cascade_flag: 'oracle.py accesses engine_b._OPENGREP_CANDIDATES, _ASTGREP_CANDIDATES, _METRIC_CANDIDATES, _GITLEAKS_CANDIDATES, _resolve_binary, _binary_version directly; any extracted sibling must re-export these names from engine_b or oracle.py must be updated to import them from the new module.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for the command-family split boundary and explicitly name the _cli/__init__.py flat import block (`from rebar._cli._llm_commands import ...`) as a cascade point that must be updated to pull entrypoints from the new module(s).
findings.finding: The plan names the _llm_commands.py split approach ('split by command family') but provides no positive rationale for WHY the command-family boundary is the right seam — in particular why families rather than, say, keeping shared infrastructure in _llm_commands.py as a pass-through/re-export hub. Without a stated rationale the implementer also has no guidance on what to do with the flat name-import in _cli/__init__.py (`from rebar._cli._llm_commands import (_criteria, _explain, _llm, _prompt, _review, _review_code, _review_plan, _scan_spec, _verify_completion)`) which imports all nine public entrypoints from a single module location — a concrete cascade the plan does not mention.
findings.location: Remediation — '_llm_commands.py — split by command family'; src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py line 24-34

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add grounding/__init__.py and its `from .engine_b import ScanResult` re-export to the cascade_flag, and explicitly constrain that ScanResult must remain in (or be re-exported from) engine_b regardless of which cluster is extracted.
findings.finding: src/rebar/grounding/__init__.py re-exports ScanResult directly from engine_b (`from .engine_b import ScanResult`) and lists engine_b as a named module in __all__. If ScanResult is extracted to the sibling, this re-export breaks. The plan does not mention grounding/__init__.py as a consumer to update.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to cascade_flag: 'grounding/__init__.py re-exports ScanResult from engine_b — ScanResult must stay in engine_b or be re-exported from it post-split.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add test-file consumers (test_criteria_eval.py, test_code_review_ws5.py, test_engine_b.py, test_conformance.py, test_polyglot_corpus.py) to the cascade_flag or a new 'test-file consumers' section, and add an AC confirming the full unit-test suite passes after each split.
findings.finding: tests/unit/test_criteria_eval.py imports _criteria directly from rebar._cli._llm_commands (three call-sites), and tests/unit/test_code_review_ws5.py + tests/unit/grounding/test_engine_b.py + test_conformance.py + test_polyglot_corpus.py monkeypatch private symbols on engine_b. These test-file consumers are not enumerated in the plan, yet they will break if the targeted symbols move to split modules.
findings.location: Remediation / cascade_flag; AC: 'Import paths / CLI command registration preserved'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly where the shared helpers (_add_ref_source, _gate_source_error, _render_source_line, _render_review_text, _render_verdict_text, _render_plan_review_text) land after the command-family split, ensuring the chosen location satisfies the no-file-<100-LOC constraint.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify where the shared cross-family helpers in _llm_commands.py (_add_ref_source, _gate_source_error, _render_source_line, _render_review_text, _render_verdict_text, _render_plan_review_text) land after the command-family split. These helpers are consumed by multiple command families. Without a stated landing zone, the implementer may (a) duplicate them across split files, (b) place them in one family file and import across families creating an awkward dependency, or (c) create a small shared-helpers fragment that violates the no-file-<100-LOC AC.
findings.location: Remediation — '_llm_commands.py — split by command family'; AC line 'No file <100 LOC created by the split'
findings.suggested_fix: Keep shared helpers in one of the larger split files (e.g. the review-family file, since it uses the most of them) and import from there, or retain them in _llm_commands.py as a thin shared-helpers residual — provided that residual stays ≥100 LOC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC2, e.g. `python -m pytest tests/ -x` (or a dedicated import smoke test) plus a CLI registration check such as `python -m rebar --help | grep <split-command-names>`, and reference these in the AC.
findings.finding: AC2 ('Import paths / CLI command registration preserved') has no concrete proving command or described verification step — no test invocation, no grep/import check, and no CLI smoke test is mentioned anywhere in the plan, leaving this criterion unverifiable beyond manual inspection.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Import paths / CLI command registration preserved'
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC2 to read: 'Import paths / CLI command registration preserved — verified by: (a) `python -m pytest tests/ -x` exits 0; (b) `python -c "from rebar._engine import engine_b; from rebar._cli import _llm_commands"` exits 0; (c) `python -m rebar --help` output contains all pre-existing subcommand names.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether 'one detector/helper cluster' extraction is sufficient to bring engine_b.py into the 200-500 LOC band, or specify that multiple clusters / a larger extraction may be required to satisfy AC #1.
findings.finding: The remediation for engine_b.py prescribes extracting 'one detector/helper cluster,' but the file sits at 800 LOC and the AC requires the 200-500 LOC band — meaning ~300-600 LOC must move. A single cluster extraction may fall short of this target, creating a silent inconsistency between the prescribed action and the measurable outcome.
findings.location: Remediation section — engine_b.py bullet; AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'extract one detector/helper cluster' with explicit guidance: 'extract sufficient clusters so that engine_b.py lands in 200–500 LOC — this will likely require moving ~300–600 LOC, possibly across more than one sibling module; each sibling must also satisfy the >100 LOC floor.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider splitting into two child tickets — one for `engine_b.py` (grounding layer) and one for `_llm_commands.py` (CLI layer) — since each split is independently valuable, testable, and releasable with no ordering dependency between them.
findings.finding: The ticket bundles two independently releasable splits across genuinely unrelated subsystems: `engine_b.py` (grounding/engine layer) and `_llm_commands.py` (CLI command layer). Each split delivers a complete, independently testable, and mergeable outcome — the grounding-engine split requires zero knowledge of the CLI layer and vice versa, different importers are audited, and different command-family concerns are validated.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific cluster to extract from engine_b.py (e.g., 'the detector helpers around lines X–Y into `engine_b_detectors.py`') so the executor has a stated default rather than an open design choice.
findings.finding: The specific split seam for engine_b.py is left entirely to the executor ('extract one detector/helper cluster') without naming the cluster, making the remediation step a placeholder design decision rather than a stated default.
findings.location: Remediation — 'engine_b.py — extract one detector/helper cluster into a sibling module'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit strategy for import-path continuity: either (a) re-export relocated symbols from the original module during a transition window (expand-contract), or (b) confirm all callers are updated atomically and describe the rollback step if CI fails mid-split.
findings.finding: The plan acknowledges that relocating functions across module boundaries breaks import paths (cascade_flag), and AC2 says 'Import paths / CLI command registration preserved,' but no rollback path, expand-contract sequence, or re-export shim strategy is described for consumers that import from the original module locations.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Migration & rollback' subsection: for each split, keep the original module as a thin re-export shim (e.g. `from .engine_b_detectors import *`) until all importers are updated and verified in CI, then remove the shim in a follow-up commit. State explicitly that if CI fails after the split, reverting the shim deletion is the rollback path.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
